title: A study of the dimer-trimer crossover in a driven three-component Fermi gas
authors: Ronnie Slowinski, Gaël Servignat, Frédéric Chevy, Carlos Lobo
abstract: We develop an Effective Field Theory (EFT) for a system with three distinguishable atomic species and present a variational calculation of the two and three-body binding energies in vacuum and in the presence of a single Fermi sea. Specifically, we consider the case where the interaction between first two atomic species is externally driven so as to produce a (non-universal) closed-channel dimer whose coupling can be controlled independently of all other interactions. We then model the remaining interactions as a contact interaction between the dimer and a third atomic species which forms the medium. We derive analytical expressions for the dimer and trimer binding energies in vacuum and in medium, and in the latter case we predict a crossing between the dimer and trimer branches as a function of the atom-dimer scattering length, analogous to the usual polaron and molecule problem. Furthermore, we show that the position of this crossing can be controlled by varying the atom-atom coupling that results from the external drive and we discuss the implications of these findings.
summary: This paper uses effective field theory to study the transition between dimer and trimer states in a three-component Fermi gas. It reveals that external driving can control the crossover between these states and that many-body effects can create trimer states that do not exist in a vacuum. This work is significant for understanding the stability and manipulation of complex clusters in ultracold gases.
main_problem: Investigating the crossover between dimer and trimer states in a three-component Fermi gas, specifically how external drives and many-body effects influence the stability and binding energies of these states.
main_result: The study demonstrates a guaranteed crossover from a dimer to a trimer ground state as the atom-dimer interaction is tuned, and shows that many-body effects can induce trimer solutions (Cooper pairs) even in regimes where they are absent in vacuum.
method: The authors use an Effective Field Theory (EFT) and a variational approach, employing Feynman rules, the T-matrix formalism, and renormalization to solve self-consistent energy equations.
model_or_system: A three-component Fermi gas consisting of three distinguishable atomic species (a, b, and c). Species a and b interact via an external drive to form a non-universal closed-channel dimer, which then interacts with a Fermi sea of the third species (c) via a contact interaction.
key_observables: Two-body and three-body binding energies, atom-dimer scattering length, and the energy difference between the dimer and trimer branches.
important_parameters: Atom-dimer scattering length (1/a_AD), two-body scattering length (a_2), effective range (R), and Fermi momentum (k_F).
main_assumptions: The dimer is assumed to be non-universal, and the third species is treated as a spinless fermion forming an unperturbed Fermi sea.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows Feynman rules for the effective Hamiltonian; Fig 2 shows Feynman diagrams for two-body interactions; Fig 4 shows trimer energy bounds; Fig 9 illustrates the energy difference between trimer and dimer branches across different interaction strengths.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the EFT framework and Hamiltonian, develops the two-body sector (vacuum and medium), extends the theory to the three-body sector (trimer binding energies), analyzes the dimer-trimer crossover, and discusses the implications of external driving.
why_interesting: It provides a theoretical framework for controlling many-body bound states using external drives, offering new insights into how the transition between different types of many-body clusters (like polarons and molecules) can be manipulated in ultracold atomic systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 2: The Feynman diagrams representing the two- body interaction between a (solid line) and b (dashed line) forming a closed-channel dimer d (double line) in vacuum.

FIG. 3: Diagrams required to calculate TAD for the scat- tering between a dimer d (double line) and c-particle (dotted) interacting through a contact interaction gAD. The first expression is a perturbative expansion in gAD truncated after g2 AD and the second gives the integral equation obtained after including infinite interactions. The vacuum two-body T-matrix (T2) enters the dimer propagator since d is a composite particle.

FIG. 4: A graph of atom-dimer binding energy rela- tive to the dimer binding energy (∆E = E + κ2 +) in vacuum as a function of inverse atom-dimer scattering length (1/aAD) for a selection of ab-scattering lengths (a2). The upper bound corresponds to the point dimer limit and the lower bound corresponds to the unitary dimer limit, with the shaded gray areas indicating the regions where trimers cannot form for any combination of a2 and aAD. The remaining results have been found by numerically solving Eq. (19) with effective range fixed so R = 0.1. We find that by varying 1/a2 the trimer so- lutions interpolate smoothly between these two limits.

FIG. 7: Diagrams used to calculate the two-body T- matrix between the impurities a (solid) and b (dashed) in medium ( ˜T2) up to a single p-h pair in the c-particle Fermi sea. The medium atom-dimer T-matrix ( ˜TAD) en- ters whenever the ab-dimer interacts with a c-particle forming the medium, producing a hole which is denoted by a backwards pointing (dotted) arrow.
